text: Hi and welcome to This is My Architecture. I'm Ziv and I'm here with Yotam from Nenit.
Hi Yotam, thank you for joining.
Hi Ziv, thank you for having me.
Yotam, tell me about Nenit.
Nenit builds technologies to make parents' life more manageable. Our main product is the Nenit Pro
camera, a smart baby monitor, the track baby sleep, wellness and development.
Nice, so what are you going to talk today?
We're going to talk about our video ingestion pipeline and our streaming architecture.
Nice, so let's dive into the architecture.
Sure, so the first thing that happens is that our camera is registering our system
by communicating with our control plane. The control plane stores the camera's state
in one of our RDS clusters.
Right, so you have this state in the RDS where is the camera and what happens next?
Now the camera starts recording short video segments and once the segment is ready, the camera
communicates with the control plane and asks for a S3 pre-signed URL and directly uploading it
to one of our S3 buckets.
I get it. And you mentioned that you analyze this segment of videos. How do you do that?
So what we're doing is we're using S3 notification for when new objects are uploaded to the bucket
and this notification triggers a Lambda function. The Lambda function pushes the S3 key to SQS FIFO
and now we have our video analysis service that receives these messages and downloads the videos
from the bucket and analyzes these videos. And we're using high performance GPUs to run our computer
vision algorithms and to generate insights. Nice. So you mentioned this EKS cluster with all of these
services. What scale are we talking about here? We're talking about hundreds of thousands of cameras
that generate these videos for analysis. Nice. It's a huge scale. And what kind of insights do you generate?
We generate what we call sleep insights. For example, when the baby wakes up or fell asleep,
and we also generate sleep recommendations to help parents to train their babies for better sleep.
And we store all this information in another RDS cluster. I get it. And I see a mobile application
here on the board. I guess it's for the parents. How do they use it? So the parents can use the mobile
application to view all these insights and recommendations by also communicating with the
control plane. They can also ask the control plane to playback the videos we used for the insights
generation and using another S3 pre-signed URL to directly download the videos from S3. So this is for
playback. And what about live streaming directly from the camera? So we do have live streaming
capabilities. What we're doing is when the camera and the mobile application are connected to the same
Wi-Fi network, the camera can push the streaming directly to the mobile application. And when the camera
and the mobile application are not on the same Wi-Fi network, we relay the streaming through our servers.
I get it. So it's a complex task to stream through the cloud. Can you show us how you do it?
Yes. So what happens first is that the mobile application notifies the control plane that it wants to pull the stream.
Now the control plane asks the camera to start pushing the stream. The camera queries Route 53 and gets a public
node IP that runs in our EKS cluster. The camera now can push the stream through the node and the node
forwards the stream to our video streaming service. Okay. And now the mobile application can stream back from
the service. You are using a client-side load balancing architecture here instead of using some kind of ELB.
Why did you choose that? We first used ELB for this architecture, but we wanted to find other alternatives
to reduce our costs. Okay. Thank you very much for sharing this architecture, Juttam. Thank you,
for having me. And thank you for watching. This is my architecture.
